data.content_ref: inline:{"claim": "The collapse was a consequence of a military revolution in infantry tactics and iron weaponry that democratized violence, enabling decentralized 'barbarian' groups to overwhelm the chariot-based aristocracies of the palace states.", "mechanism": "Palace civilizations monopolized violence through expensive bronze chariot corps. The proliferation of iron smelting (a cheaper, more accessible technology) and the development of massed infantry with javelins and long swords neutralized the chariot advantage. This allowed semi-nomadic groups (Sea Peoples, Dorians) and internal peasantries to destroy the palaces. The collapse was simultaneous because the military-technological equilibrium that kept elites in power shattered across the region within a single generation.", "scope": {"covers": ["Mycenaean Greece", "Hittite Anatolia", "Ugarit", "Levantine city-states"], "excludes": ["Egypt"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "The destruction layers uniformly show evidence of prolonged sieges by forces using advanced chariot tactics, not infantry assaults.", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Iron smelting technology demonstrably post-dates the collapse by at least a century in the affected regions.", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "Egypt survived because its centralized state under Ramesses III could still field and supply a professional army large enough to defeat the infantry-based Sea Peoples in a pitched battle, as recorded at Medinet Habu. The Hittites, reliant on chariots, could not adapt quickly enough."}
